zotero.abstractNote: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is a critical requirement for the safe deployment of machine learning models in real-world environments. While many existing methods rely on post-hoc analysis of pre-trained models, they often struggle to capture the nuanced features necessary to distinguish between in-distribution and OOD data. In this work, we propose a novel approach to OOD detection inspired by the concept of local neuroplasticity. By introducing a local learning mechanism that allows individual neurons to adapt to the input distribution during inference, we enable the model to dynamically adjust its internal representations. This adaptation process provides a robust signal for OOD detection, as the degree of change in the model's internal state serves as a reliable indicator of distributional shift. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across a range of benchmarks, showing that it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both detection accuracy and computational efficiency.
zotero.conferenceName: The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)
zotero.proceedingsTitle: The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)
